title: Deciding whether to get a water meter
subtitle: When a meter saves money and how to request one from your supplier
excerpt: A water meter charges you for actual consumption instead of a fixed rateable value. It saves money if the number of people in the household is fewer than the number of bedrooms. It costs more if consumption is high. The calculation takes two minutes.
sourceNotes: Ofwat consumer guidance on water meters and metered billing. Consumer Council for Water (CCW) meter switching guidance and case studies. Water UK data on average household consumption and metered versus unmetered bills.

content.text: Unmetered water bills are based on the rateable value of the property, set before 1990. Metered bills are based on how much water the household actually uses. The average UK household uses around 140 litres per person per day. Whether a meter is cheaper depends on whether actual use is above or below the unmetered allowance implied by the rateable value.

A meter is likely to save money if the number of people in the home is fewer than the number of bedrooms. A single person in a three-bedroom house will almost certainly be below the unmetered allowance. Two adults in a one-bedroom flat will probably pay more on a meter: consumption is high relative to the rateable value of a small property.
Find your current unmetered annual bill from your water supplier.
Estimate your household's water use: number of people times 140 litres times 365 gives an annual consumption in litres. Divide by 1,000 to convert to cubic metres.
Check the metered unit rate and standing charge from your supplier's tariff page. Multiply estimated cubic metres by the unit rate and add the standing charge to estimate annual metered cost.

Most UK water suppliers offer a 24-month trial period for metered billing. If the meter turns out to cost more, the household can switch back to unmetered billing within the trial window. After the trial, the meter usually becomes permanent. Contact the supplier to confirm the trial terms before requesting installation.
Why a meter changes consumption behaviour

Ofwat and the Consumer Council for Water consistently find that metered households use 10-15 percent less water than unmetered ones. The meter makes consumption visible and links it to cost. Fixing a dripping tap becomes financially motivated as well as environmentally motivated.

Several water supplier regions in the south and east of England are rolling out compulsory metering. These are areas of highest water stress. Thames Water, Southern Water, Anglian Water, and South East Water are the main operators. If you are in one of these regions, metering will eventually become mandatory.
